coredata.dc:title: Study on mechanical and tribological behaviors of GQDs @ Si3N4 composite ceramics
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Xu, Enguang
Zhao, Ziqiang
Wu, Chenjing
Zhai, Yanqing
Liu, Xingyu
Jia, Junhong
Lou, Rui
Li, Xiashuang
Yang, Wenlong
Li, Huaqiang
coredata.dc:description: Aiming to improve the mechanical and tribological performance of silicon nitride ceramic, one biomass material - nanoscale lignin as precursor was added into silicon nitride matrix. Through pyrolysis and hot pressing sintering, an overlayer (composed of the derivatives of nanoscale lignin – graphene quantum dots) was formed around the silicon nitride grains with a doping amount of 2 wt. %. The special core-shell microstructure refined the ceramic grains, and improved the mechanical properties of the composite by hindering the crack propagation and absorbing the fracture energy. When the composite with doping amount of 2 wt. % lignin slid against GH4169 in air, a lower friction coefficient of 0.22 and a lower pin wear rate of 1.74 × 10−6 mm3/(N·m) were fulfilled, which was attributed to the formation of surface layer containing GQDs and tribochemical products SiO2. In this study, a high performance green ceramic composite was prepared, and the composite possessed a very good application potential in industry such as cutting tools.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si3N4
Nanoscale lignin
Mechanical properties
Friction and wear

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Wear characteristics of Si3N4-based ceramic doping with nanoscale lignin

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2018JM5056 ), National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51905325 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M683406 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi province ( 2018JQ5201 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology (a) and X-ray diffraction pattern (b) of nanoscale lignin, and the morphology (c) and X-ray diffraction pattern (d) of Si3N4 raw powder.
XRD patterns of Si3N4-based composites doping with nanoscale lignin.
SEM image of single phase Si3N4-based composite material (SN0).

SEM images of Si3N4 matrix composites doped with different added amount of nanoscale lignin: (a): SN-1 L, (b): SN-2 L, (c): SN-3 L, (d): SN-5 L, (e): the TEM image of the cross-section of Fig.(b), (f): the enlarged figure of the selected area in Fig. (e).
The surface model of core-shell microstructure.
Raman spectra of Si3N4 matrix composites doped with different amount of nanoscale lignin.
The ID/IG and I2D/IG of the different composite material.
SEM images of the fracture morphology of the Si3N4 matrix composites (after bending strength test) with different nanoscale lignin doping amounts: (a): 0, (b): 2 wt. %.
Schematic diagram of crack propagation in the grain boundary phase.
SEM images of the fracture morphology of Si3N4 matrix composites (after bending strength test) with different nanoscale lignin doping amounts: (a): SN-3 L, (b): SN-5 L.
(a) Friction coefficient, and wear rates of (b) composite pin and (c) GH4169 disc of Si3N4-based composite material doping with nanoscale lignin against GH4169.
SEM image(a) and 3-D morphology and 2-D profile curve(b) of the worn surface of SN-2 L pin against GH4169 disc.
SEM image (a), SEM image (b), 3-D morphology and 2-D profile curve (c) of the worn surface for GH4169 disc against SN-2 L pin.
EDS analysis results: (a) the “1” area in Fig. 13 (a), (b) the “2” area in Fig. 13 (a); (c) the yellow line area in Fig. 13 (a).
Raman map and corresponded Raman spectrum of SN-2 L pin sample and GH4169 disc sample before and after wear test.
Schematic illustration of the friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of Si3N4-based ceramic doping with nanoscale lignin.
SEM images of the worn surfaces of (a) SN0 pin;(b) SN-1 L pin; (c) SN-3 L pin; (d) SN-5 L pin.
Si3N4-based ceramic composite with different doping amounts of nanoscale lignin.
Chemical composition of GH4169 (wt. %).
Relative contents of beta phase in Si3N4-based composites doping with nanoscale lignin.
The average diameter and maximum diameter of the grain of Si3N4-based composites with different nanoscale lignin additions.
Physical and mechanical properties of Si3N4-based composites doped with different amount of nanoscale lignin.
Raman parameters of SN-2 L pin specimen before and after wear test.

head.ce:title: Study on mechanical and tribological behaviors of GQDs @ Si3N4 composite ceramics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Shanxi University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics, Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710004, Shaanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aiming to improve the mechanical and tribological performance of silicon nitride ceramic, one biomass material - nanoscale lignin as precursor was added into silicon nitride matrix. Through pyrolysis and hot pressing sintering, an overlayer (composed of the derivatives of nanoscale lignin – graphene quantum dots) was formed around the silicon nitride grains with a doping amount of 2 wt. %. The special core-shell microstructure refined the ceramic grains, and improved the mechanical properties of the composite by hindering the crack propagation and absorbing the fracture energy. When the composite with doping amount of 2 wt. % lignin slid against GH4169 in air, a lower friction coefficient of 0.22 and a lower pin wear rate of 1.74 × 10−6 mm3/(N·m) were fulfilled, which was attributed to the formation of surface layer containing GQDs and tribochemical products SiO2. In this study, a high performance green ceramic composite was prepared, and the composite possessed a very good application potential in industry such as cutting tools.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with traditional metal materials，the ceramic materials are well known by the outstanding hardness, high strength, excellent chemical stability, thermal conductivity and corrosion resistance, which make them become the darling of cutting tool [1]. However, when the single-phase Si3N4 ceramic tools process difficult-to-machine materials (such as nickel-based superalloys and titanium alloys), they occasionally experience cracks, sticking and broken edges. It is generally attributed to inherent brittleness and poor dry tribological properties [2–4]. Hence, studies are always focused on the synergistic toughening and friction reduction of ceramics.

With the further study of graphene, scholars have tried to simultaneously improve the mechanical and tribological properties of Si3N4 ceramic materials by adding graphene materials into the ceramic matrix. Pawel Rutkowski [5] studied the Si3N4 based composites containing up to 10 wt. % graphene nanoflakes (GNP). For the ceramics, the addition of 1 wt. % of GNP phase cased a good mechanical properties (e.g. 876 MPa for bending strength and 8.6 MPa·m1/2for fracture toughness), a slightly enhanced wear resistance (10−6 mm3/ N·m for wear rate), and not so ideal anti-friction performance (0.65–0.75 for friction coefficient). The authors also indicated that, higher content of GNP would deteriorate the mechanical performance of the composite, which was related to the agglomeration of graphene and the weak bonding interface between GNP and ceramic matrix. In addition, Zhang Jinbao [6] directionally prepared Si3N4/TiC based composite ceramic containing 5 wt. % graphene platelets with the different orientations, and studied the friction and wear performance of the composite ceramic against 45 hardened steel. The results indicated a lower friction coefficient of 0.46 and wear rate of 4.29 × 10−6 mm3/N·m for the composite with 30°graphene platelets, which were resulted from an adhered tribofilm creased by graphene and iron oxides, the combined effect of graphene pull-out and contact area between graphene and the friction surface. Moreover, Pavol Hvizdoˇs [7] found that the addition of graphene platelets did improve the wear resistance of Si3N4 ceramic, while did not lower the friction coefficient. The authors indicated that, the graphene platelets seemed to be integrated into the matrix very strongly and they did not participate in the lubricating process. It can be seen from the available data, the agglomeration and orientation of graphene phase significantly affect the mechanical and tribological properties of Si3N4 ceramic [8–10], which put forward higher request to the preparation technology of the ceramic composite. Meanwhile, the influence mechanism of the carbon phase on the friction and wear behaviors of the Si3N4 ceramic remains to be further clarified.

In recent years, as a green and renewable biomass resource, lignin has entered scholars’ vision. The latest studies suggest that, the lignin can be converted into graphene-like carbon materials through pyrolysis or catalysis methods. Sun [11] et al. reported a scalable approach for producing sulfur porous three-dimensional (3D) graphene materials from lignin and polyethersulfone films by using a facile laser direct writing technique. The results indicated that the graphene materials from lignin had regular stacked structure, numerous of pores, rough surface, and d-spacing of 0.343 nm. Similarly, Yu [12] and Wang [13] also prepared graphene materials using black liquor lignin and calcium lignin sulfonate, respectively. What is more interesting, some scholars have recently prepared graphene quantum dots using lignin materials as precursor. Liu et al. [14] reported a phosphorous acid assisted hydrothermal process to produce lignin-derived graphene quantum dots, whose lattice fringes closed to the (020) facet of graphitic carbon with 0.251 lattice spacing. And, the particle size distribution was in the range of 4–6 nm. At the same time, Ding et al. [15] synthesized single-crystalline graphene quantum dots functionalized with oxygen- and nitrogen-groups through oxidized cleavage and aromatic refusion processes using alkali lignin, and the graphene quantum dots presented a hexagonal graphene network structure with a thickness of 13 at. layer, a layer spacing of only 0.21 nm and a narrow size between 2 and 6 nm. It is worth noting that, the graphene quantum dots, as a new nanomaterial, are mainly explored and studied as functional materials in recent years [16–18], while a little research focuses on its influence on mechanical and tribological properties of matrix materials when the graphene quantum dots as an incorporation phase. We tried to dope the nanoscale lignin as precursor into Si3N4 materials, and surprisingly found that graphene quantum dots could be derived from nanoscale lignin during the preparation process [19]. But, the effects of nanoscale lignin content on the species of derivatives, mechanical properties and tribological properties of ceramic materials have not been entirely clear.

Therefore, the nanoscale lignin with different content (of 1 wt. %, 2 wt. %, 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %) was doped into Si3N4 raw powders in this study, and then the silicon nitride-based composites were prepared by pyrolysis and hot-pressing sintering process. The effect of the doping amount of nanoscale lignin on the microstructure and physical and mechanical properties of the Si3N4-based composite was explored, and the strengthening and toughening mechanisms of an overlayer composed of graphene quantum dots – derivative of lignin were also studied in this study. Meanwhile, the tribological characteristics and wear mechanism of the ceramic composite against GH4169 alloy (one difficult-to-machine nickel-base superalloy) were investigated, and the influence of the surface layer containing graphene quantum dots and tribochemical products SiO2 was then revealed. In fact, it is the first study to utilize the lignin derivatives as reinforcing and toughening phases of ceramic materials. This study provides a new approach for the value-added use and potential applications of lignin-derived carbon materials in tribology material field.

In this study, the nanoscale lignin was selected as the precursor just because its good activity and dispersity, and the preparation of nanoscale lignin was mainly attributed to Prof. Lou Rui in our research group [20]. The particle size of nanoscale lignin was at a range of 50–150 nm, and this nanoscale lignin contained rich guaiacyl subunits and very few aryl ether bonds. At the temperature range from 200 °C to 600 °C, the thermal weight loss of nanoscale lignin can reach 57.27 %.

The commercial high purity Si3N4 powder (D50 = 0.7 µm, purity > 99.99 %, HeFei Aijia New Material Science & Technology, Ltd, China) was used in the present study. High purity Y2O3 (average particle size of 0.37 µm, purity > 99 %) and Al2O3 (average particle size of 1.17 µm, purity > 99 %) were used as sintering additives. The composition of Si3N4-based material with sintering additives was determined on the final derivation of carbon phase after full pyrolysis conversion of nanoscale lignin, and designed as samples SN0, SN-1 L, SN-2 L, SN-3 L, SN-5 L with different doping amount of nanoscale lignin, as shown in Table 1.

As mentioned in our previous study [19], the composite materials were prepared with two steps: powder mixing and preparation. At the first step, the different kinds of starting powders were mixed with alcohol via ball milling for 3 h at a revolution speed of 150 r/min, where the ball mill tank and grinding body were both made of zirconia. And then, the mixed powders were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 8 h and sieved through an 80-mesh stainless steel screen. At the second step, the mixed powders were placed in graphite mold in a hot press sintering furnace (HIGH MULTI 5000, Japan), and then were heated to 800 °C (which is the theoretical pyrolysis temperature of nanoscale lignin) for 30 min under nitrogen protection. Subsequently, the mixed powders were continuously heated up to 1700°C and held under a pressure of 30 MPa for 1 h. After that, the as-sintered samples (with a size of Φ45 mm×5 mm) were machined into cubes for mechanical properties test and friction test.

The bulk density and apparent porosity of the as-sintered samples were measured using Archimedes method in distilled water. The sintered samples were ground and cut with a diamond saw into bars of 3 mm × 4 mm × 35 mm, and then were mirror polished using diamond slurries in order to carry out the three-point bending strength measurement that was determined by an Electromechanical Universal Testing Machine (WDW-E, Jinan Sichuan Testing equipment Co., LTD) under a speed of 0.5 mm/min with a span of 30 mm at room temperature. The sintered samples were also processed into bars of 2 mm × 4 mm × 22 mm for fracture toughness measurement. The fracture toughness of the compacts was examined by the three-point loading and single-edge notched beam technique with a notch of 2.0 mm depth, a span of 16 mm and a speed of 0.05 mm/min. Hardness was measured by the Vickers indentation method (HV-1000A, Laizhou Huayin Instrument Equipment Co., LTD) at a load of 9.8 N for a dwell time of 15 s on the mirror-polished surface.

Dry sliding tests of the Si3N4-based materials were carried out on a pin-on-disc tribometer (MMW-1, Jinan Zhongchuang Industrial Test System Co., LTD, China) at room temperature. The pin samples with a size of 5 mm × 5 mm× 15 mm were cut from the Si3N4-based materials. The pin surfaces were polished down to surface roughness below Ra 0.08 µm. The disc sample with a size of 44 mm in diameter and 5 mm in thickness were made from the commercial GH4169 bars (Steel Special Steel Co., Ltd., China, density=8.24 g/cm3, hardness=HBS363, γ(Ni, Fe) phase) to be used as counterparts. The chemical composition of GH4169 is showed in Table 2. The metal disc surfaces were polished down to surface rough below Ra 0.05 µm.

Both the pin and disc samples were ultrasonically cleaned in the fresh alcohol. The disc is stationary, while the ceramic pin is rotated with a speed of 1000 r/min (1.73 m/s). A normal load of 10 N (0.4 MPa) was applied in all the tests. Each test was run over a period of 10000r (10 min, 1038 m). After the friction test, the pin and disc samples were cleaned by ultrasonic wave for two minutes to remove the debris floating on the surface. The initial running-in was not accounted for the calculation of the friction coefficient (f) and wear rate (w) in the steady state. The friction coefficient (f) is directly determined by the tester. The wear rate (w) is defined by w
= Δm/(ρPL), where Δm represents the mass wear volume assessed by weight loss using a microbalance (accuracy = 0.1 mg), P is the normal load, L is the sliding distance, and ρ is the density. Friction coefficients and wear rates were obtained from the average of the values taken from three runs.

The phases of the mixed raw powders and as-sintered compacts were characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/max2200 PC, Rigaku, Japan), where the target material was Cu target. The morphologies of the mixed powder, the initial surface, the microstructure, the fracture and the worn surface of the composites were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Q45, America). Also, digital optical microscope (DSX510, Olympus, Japan) was applied to observe the 2-D and 3-D morphologies of the worn surface of the sliding pair. The chemical composition of the initial ceramic composites and the worn surface were analyzed by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and the micro-Raman imaging spectrometer (Thermo Fisher DXRxi, America). All the samples used in the above tests were non-corrosive. The sintered specimens were ground, polished, and chemically etched by molten NaOH for 2 min. The microstructures of the etched surface and the morphologies of the indentations were observed using an FE-SEM (SU8100, Japan) device equipped with an EDS detector. The observation samples were prepared from the etched specimen by focused ion beam (FIB) and then analyzed by FIB-SEM (FEI Scios, USA) and TEM (JEM-2800 F, Japan).

Fig. 1 shows the microstructure and phase composition of nanoscale lignin and Si3N4 powders. As observed in Fig. 1(a), the nanoscale lignin powder presents a good dispersion. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the XRD pattern of nanoscale lignin shows a broad “steamed bread peak”, indicating an amorphous structure without any crystalline phase. Figs. 1(c) and 1(d) show the morphology and XRD pattern of Si3N4 ceramic starting powders, and it can be seen that the microscale powder is mainly composed of α- Si3N4.

The XRD patterns of as-sintered Si3N4-based composites with different doping amounts of nanoscale lignin are shown in Fig. 2. SN0 sample (single phase Si3N4) mainly consists of beta phase and Y2Si3O3N4. With doping with nanoscale lignin, alpha phase is also detected in the composite materials in addition to beta phase. Meanwhile, for all the as-sintered samples, small diffraction peaks of the Y2Si3O3N4 [21,22] can be observed.

In order to confirm the relative contents of beta phase in the Si3N4-based composites doping nanoscale lignin, the relative content of beta phase is determined by the diffraction peak intensity according to Eq. (1) [23,24]:
(1)
β −
Si
3
N
4
( wt % ) =
I
β ( 101 )
+
I
β ( 210 )
I
α ( 102 )
+
I
α ( 201 )
+
I
β ( 101 )
+
I
β ( 210 )

Where, I
α(102) and I
α(201) represent the intensities of α-phase at planes (102) and (201), and I
β(101) and I
β(210) represent the intensities of β-phase at planes (101) and (210).

The calculation results for the relative contents of beta phase in the Si3N4-based composites are displayed in Table 3. It can be clearly seen that, with the dope of nanoscale lignin, the relative content of beta phase significantly decreases compared with the single phase Si3N4 (SN0). When the doping amount of nanoscale lignin is 1 wt. % or 2 wt. %, the relative content of beta phase remains around 86.8 %. As the doping content increases to 3 wt. % or 5 wt. %, the relative content of beta phase decreases to 61.4 % or 77.6 %. Apparently, the dope of nanoscale lignin into Si3N4 starting powder inhibits the phase transition from alpha to beta in the sintering process for the ceramic composites.

The composite samples are all etched in molten NaOH for 2 min, and then their microstructures are observed by FE-SEM. The microstructure of pure Si3N4 (SN0) is shown in Fig. 3. It can be seen that, the columnar crystals with smooth surfaces crisscross each other. The microstructures of Si3N4-based composites with different doping amount of nanoscale lignin are observed by HR-TEM besides FE-SEM. With 1 wt. % doping amount of nanoscale lignin, some overlayer with the aggregation of dot-like matter is observed around the long columnar grain (indicated by red arrow in Fig. 4(a)). With 2 wt. % doping amount of nanoscale lignin, a distinct core-shell structure is observed in Fig. 4(b), and relatively complete overlayer with the aggregation of dot-like matter is also observed around the columnar grain (indicated by red arrow in Fig. 4(b)). With 3 wt. % and 5 wt % doping amounts, as shown in Fig. 4(c) and (d), the proportion of long columnar grains (β-Si3N4) significantly reduces (which is consistent with the XRD results), and the core-shell microstructure are not observed. Especially, some large mass with lamellar structure forms by the aggregation of the grains in SN-3 L composite, and some abnormally grown grains could be also observed in Fig. 4(c). Meanwhile, some tiny pores are observed on the grain surfaces for SN-5 L sample (indicated by red arrow in Fig. 4(d)).

At the same time, the average diameter and maximum diameter of the grain for the composites show a trend of first decreasing and then increasing as shown in Table 4. When the doping amount of nanoscale lignin reaches 2 wt. %, the average diameter of 0.308 µm and maximum diameter of 0.570 µm are lowest. It can be concluded that, the uniform fine grains are related to the interesting core-shell structure.

In order to further analyze the core-shell microstructure for SN-2 L composite, a cross section of the grain with complete overlayer in Fig. 4(b) is thinned by the FIB focused ion beam, and then observed by the field emission transmission electron microscope. As shown in Fig. 4, the rim or edge of the columnar grain is covered with a large amount of black dot-like matter. The local enlarged detail (indicated by white frame in Fig. 4(e)) is shown in Fig. 4(f), and amounts of nanoscale black dots with high crystallinity are clearly visible. The findings are consistent with our previous work [19]. Based on our previous exploration, the mixed starting powders of nanoscale lignin underwent thermal decomposition during the preparation process of the ceramic composite with 2 wt. % nanoscale lignin, and the derivatives were the black graphene quantum dots with the size of 8.99 nm2, the average layer number of 6–8, and the layer spacing of 0.22–0.23 nm. Fig. 5 shows the surface model of core-shell microstructure, and the surface of columnar crystals is coated with multiple layers of graphene quantum dots. So, what is the effect of nanoscale lignin doping amount on the lignin derivatives and the microstructure of composite materials?

The composites with different doping amount of nanoscale lignin are characterized by the Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6, there are the obvious characteristic peaks of Si3N4 (red dotted frame) and graphene materials in the spectra of the composites doped with 1 wt. % and 2 wt. % nanoscale lignin. When the doping amount increases to 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %, the characteristic peaks of graphene materials are still obvious, but the intensity of Si3N4 materials is weakened. Meanwhile, the intensity ratio of D to G peaks (ID/IG ratio) and the intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks (I2D/IG ratio) change with the increase of doping amount of nanoscale lignin. The calculated ratio values are shown in Fig. 7.

As shown in Fig. 7, the ID/IG ratio value decreases with the increase of lignin doping amount, and reaches the minimum at the content of 3 wt. %, while the ID/IG ratio value rises again at a content of 5 wt. %. The change of the I2D/IG ratio value is opposite. It increases with increasing of lignin doping content, and reaches the maximum at the content of 3 wt. %, while the I2D/IG ratio value decreases again at a content of 5 wt. %. According to the related studies [25], we have known that the intensity ratio of D to G peaks (ID/IG ratio) of graphene materials characterized the defects degree of graphene material, and the intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks (I2D/IG ratio) characterized the layer number of graphene material. Therefore, it can be concluded that, the defect degree and layer number of graphene materials in SN-3 L composite is least, and those of composite doping with 2 wt. % are the second lowest.

Nanoscale lignin did convert to amorphous carbon at 800 °C, and the amorphous carbon could be transformed to graphene-like material at 1300–1400 °C [12–14]. In this study, when the nanoscale lignin as precursor is added into the Si3N4-based ceramic, the nanoscale lignin is converted into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) during the preparation process. It can be also observed that, the Si3N4 grains are surrounded by the derivative products (GQDs), and the atomic arrangement inside the Si3N4 grains is not affected (as shown in Figs. 4 and 6). Namely, the derivatives of nanoscale lignin encases the Si3N4 grain, and the overlayer inhibits the dissolution of α phase Si3N4 in liquid phase and the precipitation of β-Si3N4. So, with the increase of doping amount of lignin, the phase transition of silicon nitride gradually becomes incomplete (as shown in Fig. 2 and Table 2).

Meanwhile, it can be also seen that, the physical morphology, defects and layers of graphene quantum dots vary with the amount of nanoscale lignin. When the doping amounts of nanoscale lignin are 1 wt. % and 2 wt. %, an overlayer composed of the derivative products (GQDs) surround Si3N4 grains. With the increase of doping amount of nanoscale lignin to 3 wt. %, the derivative products further increase. Then, the products (GQDs) aggregate to form a thicker coating around grains. Because the GQDs overlayer hinders the flow of liquid phase during the sintering process, there is less liquid phase exists around some grains, thus hindering the phase transformation of α-Si3N4 and causing the uneven distribution of the liquid phase. In the area with less liquid phase, the densification should delay, leading to the abnormal growth of grains [26]. The densification is slowed down and the grain growth time is prolonged [28]. So, the abnormal growth of the grains occurs for SN-3 L composite (as shown in Fig. 4(c)). When the doping amount of nanoscale lignin reache 5 wt. %, the further aggregation of more derivatives results in uneven overlayer around grains, and abnormal growth of grain is more significant. But, the inhibition effect of phase transition is weakened. In general, it is worth noting that, a uniform and dense overlayer surrounds Si3N4 grains in the SN-2 L composite, and forms a “core-shell” structure. Simultaneously, microcracks [27] in the cooling process, accelerate the cooling rate, and inhibit the grain excessive growth. So, combining these factors together, when the addition amount of nanoscale lignin reaches 2 wt. %, the grain size of Si3N4 is more uniform, the abnormal grains are less, and the densification effect is the best, as shown in Table 4. It is conducive to the improvement of hardness and bending strength of composites.

Table 5 shows the physical and mechanical properties of Si3N4-based ceramic doped with nanoscale lignin. It can be seen from Table 5 that, with the increase of doping amount, the mechanical properties show a trend of increasing first and then decreasing. Meanwhile, the SN-2L composite shows the most excellent comprehensive mechanical properties，which is consistent with the above analysis. The hardness, bending strength and fracture toughness of the sample SN-2L can reach 18.36 ± 1.52 GPa, 988.98 ± 18.77 MPa and 9.08 ± 0.23 MPa·m1/2, respectively.

Fig. 8 shows the fracture morphologies of SN0 and SN-2 L after the three-point bending test. It can be seen that, the main fracture mechanism of single-phase Si3N4 is composed of intergranular fracture (1), transgranular fracture (2) and grain pull-out (3). The grain fracture presents a clean and regular morphology (Fig. 8(a)). As shown in Fig. 8(b), from the fracture morphology of the bending strength sample SN-2 L, the grain of transgranular fracture is surrounded by a “shell” (indicated by the red arrow), and the “shell” is higher than the fracture position of grains. It can be concluded that, when the SN-2 L composite material is subjected to the external stress, on the one hand the initiated cracks must pass through “shell” as well as the Si3N4 grain, and the GQDs shell would absorb the fracture energy because of its unique microstructure [28]; on the other hand, the residual stress (originated from the mismatch of thermal expansion coefficient between GQDs “shell” and ceramic grain) offsetes the partial fracture energy. In this case, GQDs “shell” actes as a “barrier”, and the primary crack deflects and attenuats to microcracks, as shown in Fig. 9, similar to the solid solution of TiC grains in WC grains by Sun [27]. The TiC grains can change the direction of the main crack and induce the generation of micro-cracks, thereby relieving the stress concentration at the crack tip and absorbing the fracture energy, so as to achieve strengthening and toughening. In this way, SN-2 L composite with fancy “core-shell” structure possesses higher strength and toughness.

As a precursor, the nanoscale lignin is converted into GQDs during the preparation process. When the doping amount of nanoscale lignin is 2 wt. %, the derivative material-GQDs surrounds the Si3N4 grains and forms a relatively complete ‘core-shell’ structure. The GQDs shell can effectively hinder the crack propagation, thus the composites obtain better fracture toughness and bending strength. When the doping amount increases to 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %, the coating layer thickens further and has a certain toughening effect. However, the significant decrease of the β- Si3N4 and the appearance of abnormal grains lead to the decrease of strength. The fracture morphologies of SN-3 L and SN-5 L composites are shown in Fig. 10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear characteristics of Si3N4-based ceramic doping with nanoscale lignin
ce:section.ce:para: The friction coefficient and wear rate of the Si3N4-based ceramic doping with nanoscale lignin against GH4169 are shown in Fig. 11. As seen in Fig. 11 (a), the doping of nanoscale lignin significantly reduces the COF of the mating pair. When the doping amount of nanoscale lignin is 2 wt. %, the friction coefficient is lowered to 0.22, which is 74 % lower than that of the single-phase Si3N4/GH4169 pair. Meanwhile, the wear rate of the ceramic composite pin presents the similar trend. It can be seen from Fig. 11(b) that, the wear rate of SN-2 L pin is the lowest (1.74 ×10−5 mm3/(N·m)). The wear rate of GH4169 disc is lower than 1 × 10−5 mm3/(N·m) when the doping amount is higher than 2 wt. % as shown in Fig. 11(c). Nanoscale lignin doping amount of 2 wt. % is beneficial to the friction properties and wear resistance of ceramic composite.

Fig. 12 shows the worn surface morphology of SN-2 L pin against GH4169. It can be seen from Fig. 12(a) (SEM image), the surface of SN-2 L pin sample is relatively smooth. A small amount of dark grey surface layer (indicated by the red arrow) and bright white adhesion layer (indicated by the yellow arrow) form on the surface. Fig. 12(b) shows the corresponding 2D morphology using digital optical microscope, and the thickness of dark grey surface layer is measured as 0.2–1.23 µm. The morphology of the worn surface for GH4169 disc is shown in Fig. 13. It can be seen from Fig. 13(a) that, the worn surface of GH4169 disc sample is mainly composed of the light grey area and the dark grey area under the backscatter mode. Fig. 13(b) shows that, the dark grey area is relatively smooth referred under the secondary electron mode. 2-D morphology of the worn surface shows the wear surface is covered by a black surface layer, and its thickness is about 28.5 µm (Fig. 13(c)). Obviously, one dark grey surface layer forms in the wear interface between SN-2 L pin and GH4169 disc.

EDS analysis is carried out on the light grey area “1” and the dark grey surface layer “2” (Fig. 13(a)), and the results are shown in Fig. 14. It can be seen from Fig. 14(a) that the light grey area is mainly composed of Ti, Cr, Fe, Ni and other metal elements, as well as very little Si and O elements, which is very closely to the chemical composition of GH4169. Fig. 14(b) shows that, more C, Si and O elements are detected in the dark grey surface layer. The line scanning results of the boundary position between the dark grey surface layer and the light grey area (from “A” to “B”) is shown in Fig. 14(c). It is found that the atomic concentration of C and Si elements in the dark grey surface layer is observably higher than other area, and the metal element content in the dark grey surface layer is lower than the other area. Based on the above analysis, it can be preliminarily inferred that the dark grey surface layer contains amounts of C and Si elements.

The worn surface of SN-2 L pin is analyzed by Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 15. Among them, the red region represents the highest correlation with the surface of SN-2 L sample before wear. With the color changing from red to green and blue, the correlation with the original surface gradually decreases, while the correlation GH4169 gradually increases. It can be seen from the Raman map and characteristic peaks that, the red region occupies most of the wear surface area of the pin specimen (corresponding to the smooth region on the SN-2 L pin wear surface in Fig. 12(a)). The characteristic peaks show that the region is mainly composed of Si3N4, GQDs and a small amount of SiO2, and especially the D peak intensity of GQDs in the red region is significantly weaker than that of the surface of SN-2 L specimen before wear. This difference of D peak intensity means that, the GQDs on the surface changes after friction. Meanwhile, the green region occupies the local area of the worn surface of the pin sample, and its characteristic peaks show that the region is mainly composed of GH4169, Si3N4, GQDs and a little SiO2(the dark grey surface layer). The blue region has the smallest distribution area, and its characteristic peaks show that this region is mainly from the GH4169 disc sample (the bright white metal transfer layer). From the analysis results above and Fig. 12, it can be concluded that the obvious dark grey surface layer containing GQDs corresponds to the green region. Meanwhile, the tribochemical products and GQDs on the surface cover almost the entire surface corresponding to the red region in Fig. 15.

Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of GQDs before and after friction are shown in Table 6. It can be found that, the peak value of D peak decreases from 63.42 for original surface to 42.16 for worn surface, and the ID/IG ratio also decreases from 0.43 to 0.28. The transformation of peak values means that, GQDs on the surface of SN-2 L pin is not destroyed, and their defects are reduced during friction process.

Based on the above results, the modeling anti-wear mechanism of Si3N4-based ceramic doping with nanoscale lignin is proposed, as shown in Fig. 16. It can be considered that, in friction, N-GQDs forming on the surface are like a friction-reducing and anti-wear additives, which reduces friction and wear through polishing effect, repair effect and cooling effect. When SN-2 L pin slides against the GH4169 disc, the micro-bulges on the ceramic pin surface would plough the metal disc surface, and then some metal transferred layer forms on the ceramic pin surface (Fig. 12(a)). As the friction continued, a few composite ceramic materials are also transferred to the metal disc surface. During the process of repeated material transfer, the wear debris composed of GQDs, Si3N4, metal and other oxidation (e.g. SiO2) forms in the wear interface between SN-2 L pin and GH4169 disc, and then the surface layer composed of the wear debris forms on the wear surfaces of pin and disc. The surface layer composed of GQDs and tribochemical products SiO2 protects and lubricates the wear surface, avoiding direct contact between the two friction bodies, thereby reducing friction and wear losses. On the other hand, graphene-based materials possess excellent anti-friction property due to their unique crystal structure (including GQDs [29,30]), resulting in a polishing effect due to the sliding and rolling effects of GQDs. Meanwhile, GQDs in the surface layer is not destroyed and gradually aggregates under the action of shear force, which is conducive to GQDs filling in scratches to obtain a repair effect [31,32]. The roughness of the wear surface of the SN-2 L pin decreased from Ra0.239 µm before friction to Ra0.118 µm after friction. It results in the reduction of defects [33,34]. It is well known, the thermal conductivity of graphene is between 600 and 5300 W/(m·K), while the thermal conductivity of GH4169 is only between 13 and 31 W/(m·K). Obviously, GQDs in the friction surface can more rapidly conduct the surface heat during the friction process, playing a cooling effect, thus it also prevents the surface temperature rising, surface softening and adhesive wear. Therefore, SN-2 L/GH4169 pair presents the better friction and wear behaviors.

In this study, when SN0 pin slides against GH4169 disc, a large area of metal transferred layer is formed on the ceramic surface as shown in Fig. 17(a), indicating the adhesive wear for SN0/GH4169 pair. When SN-1 L pin slides against GH4169 disc, a metal layer with smaller area is found on the ceramic composite surface as shown in Fig. 17(b). When SN-2 L pin slides against GH4169 disc, no obvious continuous adhesion transferred layer is observed as shown in Fig. 13(a). Obviously, with the incorporation of nanoscale lignin precursor, the derivative N-GQDs plays an important role in inhibiting adhesion wear and lubricating wear surfaces. The morphologies of worn surfaces of SN-3 L and SN-5 L pin are shown in Fig. 17(c) and (d). There are also no obvious metal transferred layer, and some spalling pits form on the SN-3 L pin and SN-5 L pin (indicated by red arrows). Among them, in addition to the hardness and flexural strength of SN-3 L are higher than SN-5 L, the fracture toughness of SN-3 L is much higher than that of SN-5 L, so the spalling pit of SN-3 L is smaller than that of SN-5 L. In Fig. 13(b), there is a phenomenon that the spalling pit is repaired, so the wear rate of the SN-3 L pin sample is comparable to that of the SN-5 L pin sample. Maybe, the derivatives from nanoscale lignin in the ceramic composite also inhibit the adhesive wear, but the decrease of strength leads to the spalling of the ceramic composites and the formation of abrasive debris on the wear interface during the friction process. This results in a certain degree of abrasive wear. Therefore, the friction coefficients and wear rates of SN-3 L pin and SN-5 L pin rise slightly compared with those of SN-2 L/GH4169 pair.

In summary, when the nanoscale lignin doping content is 2 wt. %, the ceramic composite possesses the interesting “core-shell” structure and presents the best mechanical properties and tribological performance. It has a great potential for application in high performance cutting tools.

In this paper, nanoscale lignin is added into Si3N4 as precursor, and is derived into graphene quantum dots during the preparation process, forming a “core-shell” structure and thus significantly affecting the mechanical and tribological properties of Si3N4 ceramics. The specific conclusions are described as follows.

(1) With doping of 1 wt. % and 2 wt. %, an overlayer of graphene quantum dots is formed around the Si3N4 grains, which further refines the grains. With content of 3 wt. % lignin, the derived graphene quantum dots aggregates to a much thicker overlayer, and some abnormally grown grains appears locally. with then content of 5 wt. % lignin, more derivatives aggregate to form the overlayer with not uniform in thickness around the Si3N4 grains, and the grain size becomes more uneven. In general, with doping amount of 2 wt. %, a uniform and dense overlayer formed around Si3N4 grains, forming a fancy “core-shell” structure.

(2) With doping amount of 2 wt. % nanoscale lignin, the ceramic composites exhibit the best comprehensive mechanical properties: the hardness, bending strength and fracture toughness can reach 18.36 ± 1.52 GPa, 988.98 ± 18.77 MPa and 9.08 ± 0.23 MPa·m1/2, respectively. The dense overlayer composed of derived graphene quantum dots could effectively hinder the crack propagation, absorb more fracture energy, and imrpove the toughness and strength. When the doping amount increases to 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %, the overlayer is further thickened and also has a certain toughening effect, but the significant decrease of the proportion of β-Si3N4 and the appearance of abnormal grains lead to an obvious decrease of its strength.

(3) SN-2 L/GH4169 sliding pair shows the excellent tribological performance in air: 0.22 of friction coefficient and 1.74 × 10−6 mm3/(N·m) of the pin wear rate, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of the formation of a surface layer containing GQDs and amorphous SiO2, including polishing effect, repair effect and cooling effect. When the doping amount increases to 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %, more derivatives from nanoscale lignin inhibits the adhesive wear, but a significant decrease of strength causes the spalling of the surface during the friction process, resulting in the higher friction coefficient and wear rate of the pin.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2018JM5056), National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51905325), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M683406) and Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi province (2018JQ5201).
